segments.s199-g201: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is a 3rd-person past simple 'he heard' (in epic Persian the short past form). پیران یلان را is the direct object phrase: پیران qualifies یلان ('the elder/veteran heroes') and را marks it as definite/object. بخواند (ب + خواند) here is a 3rd-person past action meaning 'he called/summoned' (archaic prefix ب- appears in classical usage).
segments.s199-g202: ز is the poetic preposition (like از) introducing the addressee/location; لشکر فراوان is a noun phrase 'the numerous/vast host'. سخنها is plural 'words/speeches' (object), placed before the verb as is common in poetry. براند is a past 3rd-person verb from راندن meaning 'he sent/uttered/sent forth' (here 'uttered' or 'addressed').
segments.s199-g203: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('that' / 'for'). در رزم gives the locative 'in battle'. ما را is the dative/possessive construction 'for us' (classical Persian uses ما را + نبود to mean 'we had/there was for us'), and چنین دستگاه is the noun phrase 'such a formation/array' with چنین = 'such' modifying دستگاه = 'system/arrangement'.
segments.s199-g204: نبودست is an archaic past-negative construction 'it did not exist' (نبود + archaic enclitic -ست). هرگز is the adverb 'never' modifying the verb. بایران here qualifies سپاه and is used figuratively ('barren/sterile' → 'worthless/ineffective'); سپاه is 'army/host'. Word order puts the verb before the subject/phrase for poetic effect.
segments.s199-g145: چنین گفت is a formulaic opening meaning “thus (he) said.” کامد is an archaic/past-form verb “came/arrived” placed immediately before the title and name (سپهدار طوس) — a poetic inversion: literally “thus said (the) came commander Tous,” i.e. “thus said the commander Tous when he arrived.” سپهدار is a title “commander” modifying طوس.
segments.s199-g146: This short clause is elliptical/telegraphic: ابا here carries the sense of reluctance/refusal (a noun/verb meaning ‘refused/reluctant’), followed by a terse description of the state of forces: لشکری گشن = “an army (that is) hungry,” و = “and,” پیلان کوس = “the elephants’ war‑drums.” The syntax lists causes/reasons or conditions after the reported refusal; the main verb of the reported speech is omitted (poetic ellipsis).
segments.s199-g147: A nominal series: پلاشان (lit. “the ragged/ those in tattered cloaks”) و آن نامداران (“and those famed/renowned ones”) مرد here functions as the noun “men/warriors” (not the verb ‘died’); these nouns are the topic/subjects carried over to the verb in the next segment.
segments.s199-g148: بخاک اندر آمد is a verbal phrase “came to the earth / came down to the ground” (بـ + خاک + اندر + آمد), with the subject (those men) left before the verb in the previous segment — a common poetic order. سرانشان (“their heads”) is the internal subject/affected part, and ز + گرد (“from dust”) is a poetic circumstantial phrase — “from the dust,” i.e. fallen to the ground/overthrown.
segments.s199-g149: همه مرز و بوم is a noun phrase “all the borders and the land / the whole realm,” placed before the verb for emphasis. آتش اندر زدند is a past plural verbal clause meaning “they put/struck fire into / they set fire to” — زدند is a plural past tense verb; اندر marks location/into.
segments.s199-g150: فسیله سراسر modifies the following verb: فسیله (an adverbial noun meaning a respite/interval or stylistically ‘at intervals’) + سراسر “throughout,” giving the sense “all through/no respite.” بهم “together/at once” modifies برزدند (past plural “they struck/beat/assailed”). The cluster describes a widespread, concerted action (they struck/beat together everywhere).
segments.s199-g151: چو + past verb (بشنید) = temporal “when he heard.” The personal subject (افراسیاب) follows the verb here, a common poetic order: “When Afrasiab heard this speech…” این سخن is the object “this speech/these words.”
segments.s199-g152: غمی گشت is a simple predicate “he became sorrowful/was filled with grief.” و links the next action: بر چاره افگند بن is an idiomatic, somewhat archaic expression: افگند (threw/cast) plus بن (root/base) with بر چاره (“upon a remedy/for a solution”) conveys “he cast himself upon finding a solution” — i.e. “he set about seeking a remedy.” Word order and the verb افگند are poetic/archaic.
segments.s199-g97: چو is a subordinating conjunction meaning "when." نزدیکی دژ is a genitival phrase (نزدیکیِ دژ) "the vicinity/near-ness of the fortress." رسید is a past-tense verb "reached/arrived," and the subject اسپنوی (the steed) follows the verb, a common poetic/Old Persian word order.
segments.s199-g98: بیامد is a past-tense verb "came." خروشان functions as an adverbial/adjectival modifier meaning "roaring/neighing" describing the manner of coming. پر از + noun (پر از آب) is the construction "full of water," and روی (here) refers to the surface/face/flanks of the horse; the whole phrase modifies the subject of بیامد.
segments.s199-g99: که begins a subordinate/explanatory clause ("that/for"), and از کین is the causal phrase "out of vengeance/ because of hatred." چنین is an adverb "thus/like this." پشت برگاشتی is a verbal phrase literally "you turned your back" (پشت as the noun "back/turning away" with برگاشتی ‚past second-person form conveying 'you turned/removed/turned away'), expressing the action of abandoning.
segments.s199-g100: بدین (ب + این) functions adverbially as "in/with this" or "thus/in this place," and دژ is "fortress." مرا is the object pronoun "me" (object of the clause) and را is the object marker emphasizing that object. خوار is a predicative adjective "humiliated/despised," and بگذاشتی (past, second-person) from گذاشتن means "you left/you caused (to be)" — together خوار بگذاشتی = "you left (me) humiliated/you treated me as despised."
segments.s199-g101: سزد‌گر is an agentive/conditional expression meaning roughly "one who deems it fitting" or "if it befit (someone)." ز پس here means "from/afterward" (ز + پس = 'after that'), and برنشانی is an infinitival/verbal notion "to affix/raise/restore." مرا is the object "me" and را is the object marker; this whole segment is a conditional subordinate clause lacking its main verb, "if someone should deem it fitting to restore me afterwards."
segments.s199-g102: بدین ره is an adverbial phrase "in this way/on this road/path." بدشمن is a predicative description "an enemy/hostile (one)." نمانی is a present/subjunctive form of ماندن ("to remain") used here optatively/imperatively — "do not remain/please do not remain" in the context of a plea — and مرا (with را) is the object "toward me" (i.e. "remain my enemy"). The clause therefore pleads "do not remain my enemy in this manner."
segments.s199-g103: تژاو سرافراز را is an object-marked name/phrase (Tazhāv Sarāfraz) with را indicating the person toward whom the feeling is directed. دل بسوخت is an idiomatic intransitive clause "the heart burned," meaning "his heart was moved/suffered/was grieved/was set aflame (emotionally)." The construction places دل as the grammatical subject and بسوخت (past) as the verbal predicate.
segments.s199-g104: بکردار is an adverbial/qualifying phrase "by/with a virtuous/noble deed." آتش رخش is a genitival phrase "the (lit.) fire of the steed/horse" (آتشِ رُخْش), and برفروخت is a past verb with the prefix بر- meaning "kindled/ignited" here (not "sold"); together it says that, by a noble deed, he kindled/ignited the steed's fire (i.e. roused the horse). Word order places the object phrase before the verb, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s199-g161: کنون (adverb) opens the clause meaning 'now/presently'; نیست is the negated copula/existential ('is not') with the predicate noun phrase امروز روز درنگ ('today, a day of delay/tarrying'). Classical Persian commonly omits the separate verb 'to be', so the structure literally reads 'now is not today a day of tarrying.'
segments.s199-g162: جهان گشت is a past/resultative construction: گشت (from گردیدن) = 'became/turned', with جهان as subject. بر مرد بیدار تنگ is a prepositional phrase meaning 'narrow/close upon the wakeful man' (بر + dative-like object + predicate تنگ). Word order places the subject and verb before the prepositional complement, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s199-g163: جهاندار is the nominal subject 'the world-ruler/sovereign'. پیران هم groups 'the elders' with the sovereign: هم here functions as 'together/with' (classical use to join participants). اندر شتاب is an adverbial phrase 'in haste' (اندر = archaic form of در = 'in').
segments.s199-g164: برون آمد is a compound verb 'came out/forth' (برون preverb + آمد). از پیش here is a frozen classical expression meaning 'from before/in the presence of' (پیش = 'before; in presence of'), so the whole phrase means 'came forth before Afrasiab.' Word order places the preverb before آمد and the source phrase after it.
segments.s199-g165: ز هر مرز is an ablative phrase 'from every border' (ز is the poetic form of از). مردان جنگی is a noun phrase 'warlike men/warriors' (مردان modified by جنگی). بخواند is the narrative past/preterite used in epic style meaning 'he summoned/called' (بـ‑ prefix is the classical imperfective/past narrative form).
segments.s199-g166: This coordinate sequence lists consecutive actions: سلیح و درم داد 'he gave weapons and dirhams' (داد = past 'gave' with direct objects سلیح, درم). و لشکر براند links the next action: براند (from راندن) here means 'to drive/send/put in motion' the army — a transitive action applied to لشکر. The verbs are parallel past narrative forms.
segments.s199-g167: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. آمد ز پهلو برون is the motion phrase 'came out from the flank/side' (ز = 'from', پهلو = 'side', برون = preverb 'out'). In classical word order the verb آمد follows چو and precedes its source phrase; پهلوان (the hero) appears after the verb as the subject or appositive.
segments.s199-g168: همی is an archaic verbal particle often marking continuous or emphatic action (here giving an immediate/ongoing sense). نامزد کرد is a light-noun + verb sequence meaning 'made (himself) a nominee/candidate' or 'put himself forward'; جای گوان = 'a place/position like that' (جای = place/position, گوان an older word meaning 'like/thus'). The object phrase follows the verb, as usual.
segments.s199-g25: کنون is an adverb 'now' placed at the clause start. مرزبانم is مرزبان + the enclitic -م: in older Persian poetry that -م often serves to express the speaker's predicate ('I am marzban') without an explicit copula. بدین is the prepositional particle + demonstrative (= 'with/at this' or 'on this'), and تخت و گاه are two co‑ordinate nouns ('throne and seat') giving the location/office. Word order is adverb → predicate → locative phrase.
segments.s199-g26: This phrase continues the predicate by listing honors. نگین functions as a predicative noun 'jewel' (metaphorical), بزرگان is the genitive/possessive 'of the great ones/nobles', و links the two appositive descriptions, and داماد شاه is another title 'the king's son‑in‑law'. Together they expand on what the speaker claims to be (مرزبانم).
segments.s199-g27: بدو (to him) appears before the verb in inverted word order common in classical Persian: indirect object → verb → subject. گفت here is the past verb 'said' (the provided lemma senses list is lexical; function here is verbal), گیو is the subject 'Giv'. اینکه گفتی is a nominalized clause 'what/that you said' and مگوی is the negative imperative 'do not say' addressing that clause.
segments.s199-g28: که introduces a result/reason clause ('for/that'). تیره شود uses تیره 'dark/blemished' + شود (subjunctive/present of شدن) meaning 'becomes tarnished'. زین سخن = 'by/from this speech' (زین = 'this' + bridging particle) and آبروی is the affected noun 'reputation/honour'. The verb (شود) comes before its affected noun (آبروی), a normal arrangement in older Persian poetry.
segments.s199-g29: از ایران بتوران strings a directional/place phrase: از ایران ('from Iran') + بتوران (classical compound meaning 'to/into Turan' or 'in Turan'), followed by a relative/clarifying clause introduced by که. دارد is the present auxiliary 'has' or 'holds' and نشست can be read as either noun 'a seat/session' or verbal noun/verb 'has taken a seat/settled' — here the clause asks about someone who has set himself down (taken up a post) in Turan after coming from Iran.
segments.s199-g30: مگر introduces an exception/rhetorical qualification ('unless' / 'save that'). خوردنش is the gerund/possessive form 'his eating' (literally 'the eating of him' or 'his eating'), خون 'blood' names what is eaten, بودگر is a fused conditional particle meaning 'if it were' or 'were it that', and کبست is an obscure/archaic noun/adjective (the text preserves a rare word). Together the line poses a rhetorical possibility: unless what he consumed was blood — if he were a 'کبست' — i.e., a bleak/monstrous condition; the phrase is poetically elliptical and archaic.
segments.s199-g31: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. مرزبانی here is a nominal form meaning 'being marzban' or 'the office of frontier‑guard/commander', و links the second title داماد شاه 'the king's son‑in‑law'. The whole segment lists the high offices/relations that form the condition.
segments.s199-g32: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. بیشتر is comparative 'more' (here 'greater' or 'larger') and modifies سپاه 'army' (at the end). زین نداری is a comparative phrase 'than/from this lack/poverty' (زین = 'of this', نداری = 'lack/poverty'), so the segment asks why, given those offices, the speaker's army is nevertheless so small; the classical word order places the question word first and the head noun (سپاه) at the end.
segments.s199-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning “when.” خورشید is the subject and بر + زد is a past action (زد, 'to strike/act') with the verbal prefix بر giving an upward/aloft sense: “hoisted/raised.” ز گردون is a poetic-origin phrase meaning “from the sky/heavens,” and درفش is the direct object (‘standard, banner’) — the whole clause reads “When the sun raised the banner from the heavens.”
segments.s199-g2: دم شب is a nominal phrase meaning the tip/edge or verge of the night; شد is the past of شدن meaning “became.” از introduces the source/cause (“from”), خنجر او is a possessive construction (“his dagger” — noun + pronoun), and بنفش is a predicate adjective meaning “purple.” The clause therefore says the night’s edge became purple because of his dagger.
segments.s199-g3: غمی شد is an impersonal/predicative phrase “a grief arose/came.” دل مرد پرخاشجوی is a genitive/possessive grouping — ‘the heart of the quarrelsome (or aggressive) man’ — with the adjective پرخاشجوی qualifying مرد. The verb شد places the event in the past.
segments.s199-g4: بدانست is past “he knew/perceived.” کو را here functions as an archaic interrogative/relative meaning ‘who’ or ‘which one’ (with را marking the object). بد آمد is an idiomatic verbal phrase “ill/bad befell” (بد = unfavorable, آمد = happened), and بروی is an older form (literally written as بروی) that should be read as بر وی ‘upon him’ — i.e. “who would come to misfortune.”
segments.s199-g5: برآمد is a past verb meaning “arose/issued forth.” خروش is the noun “cry/clamor,” possessed by or associated with خروس و چکاو (the rooster and the lark). The conjunction و links the two animals; the phrase means that their cry/song was heard.
segments.s199-g6: کبوده is an adjective ‘dark‑blue’ used here nominally (“the dark/blue one”) and is the subject of نیامد, the negative past (‘did not come’). بنزد is an archaic/prepositional compound meaning ‘to the presence of / near,’ and تژاو here is a noun meaning ‘radiance/brightness.’ So the clause says that the dark‑colored one did not approach the radiance.
segments.s199-g7: سپاهی is a collective noun ‘soldiers/host.’ که بودند با او is a relative clause “who were with him” (که + past بودند). بخواند is an older/epic-form verbal form meaning ‘cried/called/sang’ (a past narrative use with a prefixed ب- common in classical texts); the verb links to سپاهی as its action.
segments.s199-g8: وزان is a compound of و + ز آن meaning “and from that,” pointing back to the place or moment just mentioned. جایگه = place/spot, تیز modifies لشکر as an adjective meaning ‘swift,’ and براند is a verbal form from راندن (‘to drive’/‘to put in motion’). The clause therefore reads “and from that spot the swift army/host put itself in motion / drove forward.”
segments.s199-g65: یکی تیره گرد از میان بردمید is a compact clause: 'یکی' is an indefinite article/particle ('a certain' / 'a'), 'تیره گرد' is a nominal phrase 'dark cloud/dust' (adjective before noun), 'از میان' is a prepositional phrase 'from the middle/among', and 'بردمید' is a past-tense verbal form meaning 'carried off/rose/was swept away'. Word order is typical Persian (NP — PP — verb) and the whole segment states a single event (a dark dust-cloud moved/rose from the center).
segments.s199-g66: بدان سان که خورشید شد ناپدید is a result clause introduced by the fixed poetic phrase بدَان سان که 'in such a manner that'. Here ب functions as a prefix/particle, دان/سان together form 'that manner' (a somewhat archaic/poetic construction), and که introduces the subordinate clause. شد is the past 'became' and ناپدید is an adjective 'unseen/vanished', so the clause means 'so that the sun became unseen.'
segments.s199-g67: جهان شد چو آبار بهمن سیاه: جهان شد is 'the world became' (شد = past of شدن). چو introduces a simile 'like/as', and آبار بهمن سیاه is a poetic image 'the (black) torrents/streams of Bahman' (آبار = torrents/waters, بهمن = avalanche/torrent image here, سیاه = black/dark). The adjective سیاه follows the noun phrase and qualifies the simile—overall: 'the world became like black torrents.'
segments.s199-g68: ستاره ندیدند روشن نه ماه is an inversion typical of poetry: the negative verb ندیدند ('they did not see') follows the object words. The phrase is parsed as 'ستاره روشن ندیدند نه ماه' — 'they saw neither star nor moon bright.' روشن is predicative 'bright', and نه before ماه negates the moon as well; the verb ندیدند is past tense (they did not see).
segments.s199-g69: بقلب سپاه اندرون گیو گرد is spatial: بقلب ('into the heart/center of') + سپاه ('the army/host') + اندرون ('within/inner part') locate the action inside the host, then گیو (proper name) modifies گرد. Here گرد is used as a noun 'cloud/whirl/dust' (the dust-whirl associated with Giv); word order places the location first and the actor/phenomenon after it.
segments.s199-g70: همی از جهان روشنایی ببرد uses همی plus a present-form verb to express a continuous/habitual or narrative action (a typical epic/past style where همی + present conveys ongoing past action). از جهان روشنایی = 'the world's light' with از marking what is taken, and ببرد is the present/subjunctive stem of بردن used in narrative to mean 'it carries/takes away' (here rendered as past narrative: 'took away' or 'was taking away').
segments.s199-g71: بپیش اندرون بیژن تیزچنگ places the subject and motion: بپیش ('forward/toward the front') + اندرون ('into the interior') indicate direction, followed by the proper name بیژن تیزچنگ (Bijan Tizchang). The phrase is a fronted adverbial of motion followed by the actor's name, a common poetic order.
segments.s199-g72: همی بزمگاه آمدش جای جنگ: همی again marks ongoing/narrative action; بزمگاه ('banquet-hall/place of revelry') is the syntactic subject/topic, آمدش is آمد + the enclitic pronoun -ش ('came to him' or 'became to him'), so literally 'the bāzm-gah came to him' i.e. 'the revel-hall became for him'؛ جای جنگ ('a place of battle') completes the predicate. This construction (X آمدش Y) is a poetic way to say 'X became Y for him.'
segments.s199-g41: ستانمت is a first‑person verb (archaic form) meaning 'I exacted/collected' and appears before its objects (verb‑first order allowed in poetry). ز is the poetic preposition 'from', followed by the objects خلعت 'robe of honour' and خواسته 'the thing asked/desired' (خواسته here functions as a noun 'that which was requested'). The conjunction و links the two objects.
segments.s199-g42: This is a coordinate noun phrase listing the specific items: پرستنده 'a worshipper/devotee' and اسپ 'steed/horse' qualified by the postpositive adjective آراسته 'adorned'. In Persian the adjective normally follows the noun, so 'اسپ آراسته' = 'an adorned horse'.
segments.s199-g43: تژاو فریبنده is a descriptive noun phrase: تژاو 'radiance/brightness' qualified by the adjective فریبنده 'deceptive/alluring'. گفت is the speech verb 'said' introducing the following vocative ای دلیر 'O brave one' (ای marks direct address). The order NP + گفت + vocative is poetic but functions as 'X said, “O brave…”'.
segments.s199-g44: درفش مرا is an older word order where the possessed noun (درفش 'standard') is followed by مرا (here marking possession 'my' or 'to me'); را is the definite/object marker attached to the following object. کس 'anyone/no one' is the indefinite subject and نیارد is an archaic verbal form meaning 'bring' (the negative sense is expressed by ن before the verb), while بزیر is an adverbial 'down'. Together: 'No one will bring my standard down.'
segments.s199-g45: مرا functions here as a dative/possessive ('for me' / 'mine' in older usage) followed by the locative ایدر 'here' and temporal اکنون 'now'. نگینست is a contracted, poetic fusion of نگین + است ('is [a] jewel' or 'is jewel‑like') and the conjunction و links to the following noun گاه 'place/time/seat'.
segments.s199-g46: This segment is an enumerative list of possessions/items: پرستنده 'a worshipper/devotee', گنج 'treasure', تاج 'crown', سپاه 'army', linked by و 'and'. These nouns are in apposition to the previous clause (they are what he has 'here now').
segments.s199-g47: همان selects/points to 'that' or 'such' and is followed by the coordinated nouns مرز 'border' و شاهی 'kingship'. چو is the comparative/simile 'like/as' introducing the proper name افراسیاب (Afrasiab): 'border and kingship like Afrasiab's.'
segments.s199-g48: کس is an indefinite pronoun here meaning 'no one' (in negative scope), این را is the object marker construction 'this' + را, ز ایران 'from/of Iran' gives the origin/group, نبیند is a negative/subjunctive form 'see (not)' and بخواب is an adverbial 'in sleep' (i.e. 'even in sleep' or 'in a dream'). The clause expresses a wish/curse that no one of Iran should even see such a thing in sleep.
segments.s199-g137: گرفتند is a past, 3rd-person plural verbal form “they seized/took.” The phrase هر یک کمندی is a quantified object (“each one [a] lasso”), with هر یک preceding the noun as in Persian 'each one a X.' بچنگ (local contraction of به چنگ) is an adverbial/prepositional-like element “into the grasp / in hand,” marking how they took the lassos. Together this segment is the main clause: “they took, each a lasso in hand.”
segments.s199-g138: چنانچون is a comparative/simile marker “just as / just like.” بود is the past copula “was,” linking the simile to the following predicate. ساز here is used in the sense of equipment/means (poetically ‘kit’), not a musical instrument, and مردان جنگ is a genitive compound “men of war / warriors” modifying ساز. The whole segment supplies the comparison: “just as was the gear of men at war.”
segments.s199-g139: بخم is an archaic/poetic adverb meaning something like “at once / suddenly.” اندر آمد is a two-word verbal expression where آمد is the past tense “came/appeared” and اندر (in/within) helps locate the action; سر بارگی is a noun phrase (“a head/crest of foliage” – سر = top/head, بارگی = foliage/leaf). Grammatically this segment describes the sudden appearance of a leafy crest or top.
segments.s199-g140: بیاراست here functions as participial/adjectival “well‑adorned / having been adorned.” لشکر is the subject “army/host.” بیکبارگی is an adverbial noun meaning “with suddenness / all at once,” modifying the verb or the manner of appearance. The phrase thus says the army, well‑adorned, appeared/advanced suddenly.
segments.s199-g141: نشستند is a past 3rd‑person plural verb “they sat / they took their seats.” بر جایگاه is a locative prepositional phrase “upon the place/seat/platform.” تژاو is an attributive/name-like noun following جایگاه; here it specifies the kind or name of the place (“the shining/brilliant place”). Together the segment states where they sat. Note: the supplied lemma/senses for نشستن appear to be mismatched to ‘نشستند,’ so the appropriate sense index is unavailable.
segments.s199-g142: سواران ایران is a genitive noun phrase “the riders/horsemen of Iran.” پر از is the adjectival construction “full of,” followed by the coordinated nouns خشم و تاو (“anger/wrath and radiance/glow”); و simply joins the two qualities. Grammatically this is an adjectival/post‑nominal description applying to the riders.
segments.s199-g143: تژاو stands as a nominative/name or epithet (here a person or titular figure). غمی is a substantive used adjectivally “sorrowful / full of grief.” با دو دیده پرآب is a prepositional phrase “with two eyes full of water,” where با = “with,” دو = “two,” دیده = “eye,” and پرآب = “water‑filled / teary.” The segment describes the person Tazhāv as grief‑stricken and tearful.
segments.s199-g144: بیامد is the past tense “came.” بنزدیک is a locative/adverbial “near / close to,” and افراسیاب is the proper name “Afrasiab.” Together this short clause indicates motion toward Afrasiab: “came close to Afrasiab.”
segments.s199-g193: میان is a preposition meaning 'between' placed before the place name; سرخس here is a place name (Sarakhs) rather than the common noun, and است is the copula linking the locative phrase to the following predicate نزدِیک طوس ('near Tus'). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: 'میان سرخس است نزدیک طوس' = 'it is between Sarakhs, near Tus.'
segments.s199-g194: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; باورد here is a place-name (Bāvord) rather than the verb 'believe' (so no listed sense fits); برخاست is a past tense verb 'rose/arose' and governs آوای کوس, literally 'the sound/cry of the drum' (آوای = 'the sound/voice of', کوس = war-drum). The clause means 'from Bāvord rose the sound of the war-drum.'
segments.s199-g195: بپیوست is a past-form verbal prefix construction meaning 'was attached/followed' or 'followed on'; here it takes گفتار ('speech/words') as its object, and کارآگهان is a genitive/attributive noun meaning 'the informed/knowledgeable' — so گفتارِ کارآگهان = 'the words of the informed.' The phrase therefore reads 'the words of the informed followed/arrived.'
segments.s199-g196: بپیران is an archaic contraction of به پیران ('to the elders'); بگفتند is the past tense 'they said' (they told); یک‌یک is an adverbial 'one by one' and نهان is an adverb meaning 'secretly' (not the adjective 'hidden' here). Literal order is 'to the elders they said one-by-one secretly.'
segments.s199-g197: که introduces a content clause 'that...'. ایشان همه is the subject 'they all'; می‌گسارند is an imperfective/present habitual verb meaning here roughly 'revel/celebrate' (poetic register) and is followed by و مست, where مست is a predicate adjective 'drunk/intoxicated.' The progressive 'می‌' prefix marks habitual/ongoing action.
segments.s199-g198: شب و روز is the temporal adverbial 'night and day'; با introduces the instrument/means 'with' and جامِ پر is a noun phrase 'a full cup' (جام = cup, پر = full). می‌بدست is an imperfective verb here meaning 'they hold/take' (continuous action), the subject continuing from the previous clause (ایشان).
segments.s199-g199: سواری طلایه is a noun phrase 'vanguard rider/horseman' (طلایه = vanguard modifying سواری). ندیدم is the 1st-person past 'I did not see'; براه is an adverbial/locative 'on the road/en route.' The object phrase precedes the verb in this poetic order: 'I saw no vanguard rider on the road.'
segments.s199-g200: نه is a negative particle 'nor/not'; اندیشه here means 'sign/trace or thought/intent' (used as 'any sign/intent of'); رزم is 'battle' and forms a genitive chain with توران سپاه ('the army/host of Turan') so the phrase reads 'nor any sign/intent of battle from the Turanian host.' The negation applies across both clauses (no rider and no battle-intent).
segments.s199-g81: همی شد here is a fossilized poetic periphrasis: همی (a continuative/adverbial particle) + شد (become) gives a past progressive/ing sense 'began/kept becoming' — so the clause says someone 'began to be' or 'kept being' something. گریزان is an adjective/participle 'fleeing' placed before the name it describes. تژاو is the proper-name/epithet being described (poetically glossed as 'radiant'), and دلیر is another postpositive adjective 'brave'; the word order is adjective(s) + name, typical in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s199-g82: پسش is an adverbial 'after him/then'; the two names بیژن and گیو appear in sequence as the agents/actors; برسان here functions as a perfect/simple past poetic form 'brought/caused to bring' (in Shahnameh verbal forms are often shortened) meaning 'brought' or 'sent to bring'; شیر is the direct object 'the lion'. Word order is: time/adverb + subject(s) + verb + object.
segments.s199-g83: This is a sequence of participial/adjectival phrases stacked as descriptors: خروشان و جوشان are parallel present-participle adjectives 'roaring/boiling' (intense, noisy), the conjunctions و simply join them, and نیزه بدست is a compound adjectival phrase 'spear-in-hand' (نیزه 'spear' + بدست 'in the hand'), placed after the adjectives, all serving to describe the (implied) subject.
segments.s199-g84: تو is the 2nd-person subject 'you'. گفتی is presented here as the speech verb 'you said' (note: the senses list in the data is dialog/nominal but the function is verbal in the line); که introduces the reported clause 'that…'. غرنده is another adjectival form 'roaring/roaringly' modifying شیرست, where شیرست is a nominal phrase 'is (a) lion' (شیر + copulative -ست), and مست is a predicate adjective 'intoxicated/drunk' (often used figuratively). The clause structure is 'You said that (the) roaring lion was drunk.'
segments.s199-g85: یکی is an indefinite subject 'one/someone/a certain (man)'. نیزه زد is the verbal phrase 'struck/struck with a spear' (نیزه as the instrument/object, زد 'struck' past). بر میان is a prepositional phrase 'into the midst/middle of' (بر + میان), and تژاو is the proper name in the object of that phrase; overall: 'Someone struck a spear into the middle of Tžāv.'
segments.s199-g86: نماند is the past negative sense 'did not remain / there remained' (here used negatively in context: 'there remained none'). آن زمان is a temporal adverbial 'at that time / then'. با + تژاو is 'with Tžāv' or 'in Tžāv' (marking whose radiance is spoken of). ایچ is an emphatic negative 'nothing/none', and تاو is 'radiance/glow'; the clause thus reads 'At that time with Tžāv there remained nothing of radiance.'
segments.s199-g87: گراینده is an agent-noun 'one who directs/turns/steers' (here likely an actor's epithet). بدبند is a compound adjective 'badly-bound / ill-fastened' modifying the following noun رومی زره 'Roman armor' (رومی 'Roman/Byzantine', زره 'armor'). The phrase groups as 'the guider/agent; the Roman's poorly bound armor' (poetic ellipsis frequently omits an explicit subject-marker).
segments.s199-g88: بپیچید is a past tense verb 'he twisted/turned' (ب‑ prefix + پیچید). و is the coordinating 'and'. بگشاد here is a past form 'he opened/loosened' or 'he made loose' (ب‑ + گشاد). بند گره is a noun phrase 'the band’s knot' (بند 'band/fetter' + گره 'knot'); the clause means 'he twisted and loosened the knot of the band.'
segments.s199-g57: A noun phrase made of two prepositive modifiers joined by و (سرافراز و بیداردل) followed by the head noun پهلوان. In Persian adjectives/epithets normally precede the noun; there is no copula here — the whole phrase stands as the subject/topic.
segments.s199-g58: به پیری = a circumstantial phrase 'in/at old age' (به + noun). نه negates the following clause: نه آنی که بودی جوان = 'not that which you were when young.' بودی is 2nd person singular past 'you were' and آنی functions pronominally ('that/the same'); the relative/connector که introduces the subordinate clause 'که بودی جوان'.
segments.s199-g59: ترا (ت + را) is the object/indirect object 'to you' (ت second‑person suffix plus object marker را); با تژاو = 'with (your) radiance/beauty'. این همه پند چیست is a question 'what is all this advice?' — چی + ست (copula) forms the interrogative copula 'what is...'. Word order is poetic: object/beneficiary before the question clause.
segments.s199-g60: بترکی is a classical/poetic conditional-ish form (ب‑ prefix + verb) meaning 'if it should burst / were to break'. چنین مهر و پیوند = 'such love and bond'. The clause ends with چی + ست again, asking 'what is (it) if such love and bond should burst?' — an evaluative rhetorical question.
segments.s199-g61: همی is an emphatic/continuous particle ('ever, always' or emphatic here); گرز و خنجر are the objects 'mace and dagger'. بباید + verb gives necessity/impersonal obligation ('it is necessary / one must'); کشید here means 'to draw/wield' (the weapon) and follows بباید in an impersonal construction — word order places objects before the verb phrase for poetic effect.
segments.s199-g62: دل و مغز ایشان = 'their heart and brain' (ایشان marking possession/third person). Again بباید introduces necessity and is followed by درید ('to rend/tear'); the construction expresses that their heart and brain must be torn — a hortative/violent prescription phrased impersonally.
segments.s199-g63: Two short clauses: برانگیخت اسپ = 'he roused/spurred the horse' (برانگیخت is 3rd‑person past transitive), و برآمد خروش = 'and a cry/roar arose' (برآمد used impersonally 'rose/issued forth' with خروش as subject). Parallel verbs describe quick successive actions.
segments.s199-g64: نهادند is 3rd‑person plural past 'they placed/put'. The objects (گوپال و خنجر) precede the verb as is common in poetry; بدوش (short for به دوش) is a locative phrase 'on the shoulder / to the shoulder', so the whole means 'they put Gopal and the dagger on (their) shoulders.'
segments.s199-g49: پرستار is a noun 'caretaker/keeper' governing the following noun phrase; the genitive relation to 'مادیانان گله' is implied (ezāfe omitted in classical/poetic style). وز here functions as an archaic connective equivalent to 'of/از' introducing the possessed herd (this sense is not given in the sense list). مادیانان گله = 'a herd of mares' (مادیانان = mares; گله = herd).
segments.s199-g50: بدشت is a locative 'on/in the plain' placed before the verb phrase. گروگرد کرده یله is a verbal/periphrastic cluster: گروگرد (a throng/crowd) + کرده (perfect/causative auxiliary 'made/caused') + یله (adjective/adverb 'loose, unrestrained'), so the phrase means 'caused a throng and set (them) loose' or 'made them run wild'. Word order places the place first, then the resultant action.
segments.s199-g51: تو is the vocative/2nd-person 'thou/you' (poetic). این اندکی لشکر من is the noun phrase 'this small host of mine' (اندکی = small/a little; لشکر = host/army; من = my/I used possessively after the noun). مبین here functions as an exhortative/imperative 'behold/see' in a poetic register (not the adjectival sense 'clear'), addressing the addressee to look at the host.
segments.s199-g52: مراجوی is a noun/participle 'those who seek/seekers' (literally 'seeker(s)' or 'those who come to seek'). با + گرز is the instrumental 'with a mace/club'; بر پشت زین is a locative phrase 'on the rear of the saddle' (بر = on; پشت = back/rear; زین = saddle). The whole segment describes the seekers who come armed with a mace on their saddles.
segments.s199-g53: The clause begins with the subject and time: من امروز ('I, today'), then the instrumental phrase با این سپاه ('with this army'), and finishes with the verb آن کنم ('I will do that'). کنَم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive form used here to express deliberate future action/intention ('I will do').
segments.s199-g54: کزین آمدن تان پشیمان کنم is a subordinate/result clause: کزین 'from this' introduces the deed; آمدن تان 'your coming' (تان = your, plural/formal); پشیمان 'regretful' is an adjective complement; کنم is causative here ('I will cause/make [you] regret'). Thus the whole means 'I will make you regret coming here.'
segments.s199-g55: چنین گفت بیژن is the straightforward narrative 'thus Bijan said'. بفرخ پدر appears as an address to the father (either the name/epithet 'Bafrakh' or a vocative), placed after the speaker; the exact function of بفرخ is lexical/onomastic here rather than the listed verbal senses, so it is not matched to the provided senses list. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said'.
segments.s199-g56: که‌ای is an interrogative/vocative 'who (are you)?' used in poetic diction (که + ای). نامور is an adjective 'renowned/famed'. گرد here is an archaic/poetic element (often part of verbal or adjectival formations meaning 'become' or linking adjectives) and does not match the literal senses listed; پرخاشخر is an adjectival/noun 'quarrelsome, aggressive one'. Together the phrase addresses someone: 'Who are you, famed and quarrelsome?'
segments.s199-g113: The phrase begins with the proper name تژاو (Tazhav) followed by the temporal adverbial آن زمان (“at that time / then”). با + noun here marks the indirect object ('to/with the devotee'): با پرستنده گفت = 'said to the devotee.' گفت is a past-tense verb (classical/persian past 'said') and closes the clause.
segments.s199-g114: که introduces reported speech or a subordinate clause ('that'). دشوار کار آمد is literal word order adjective–noun–verb: 'a difficult task came' = 'a difficult task arose.' آمد is simple past. The final ای خوب جفت is a vocative: ای = 'O', خوب qualifying جفت 'pair' (addressing two beings).
segments.s199-g115: فروماند is a poetic/past participial/intransitive form meaning 'became exhausted/was worn out.' The demonstrative این ('this') immediately modifies اسپ جنگی ('war-horse' — noun + adjective), and ز کار gives the cause ('from/because of work/toil').
segments.s199-g116: ز پس = 'after that/thereafter' (ز = poetic 'from/of', پس = 'after/then'). بدسگال آمد is 'an ill‑omened/ill‑fated one came' (آمد past). و پیش غار places the arrival spatially: پیش 'before/in front of' + غار 'the cave.'
segments.s199-g117: اگر introduces a conditional 'if.' دور از ایدر is an adverbial phrase 'far from here' (دور + ز/از + ایدر). به بیژن is the directional phrase 'to Bijan.' رسم here is a classical/poetic verb form meaning 'I come/arrive / I reach' (not the modern noun), so the clause reads 'if I go far from here to Bijan / if I reach Bijan.'
segments.s199-g118: بکام functions adverbially 'to the liking / to the advantage (of).' بداندیش modifies دشمن: 'the ill‑minded / malicious enemy.' The final رسم again is the classical verbal sense 'comes to pass / takes effect'—the line says that the enemy's malicious plan will come to fruition if the speaker reaches Bijan.
segments.s199-g119: ت + را together yield ترا ('you' as object/topic marked by را). نیست is the existential/negation 'is not / there is not.' دشمن is the noun 'enemy,' and بیکبارگی is an adverbial noun 'suddenness / all at once.' The clause thus says 'There is not a sudden enemy for you' (i.e., 'an enemy will not attack you suddenly').
segments.s199-g120: بمان is the imperative 'stay / remain.' تا here means 'until / so that' and governs the purpose/subordinate clause that follows: برانم من این بارگی = 'that I may drive away this burden/problem.' برانم is a subjunctive/optative form ('I may drive/drive away'), من is the first‑person pronoun for emphasis, and این بارگی uses بارگی in a figurative sense 'this burden/trouble' (not literal foliage).
segments.s199-g73: وزان سوی is a prepositional phrase meaning 'from that side/direction' (وزان = 'from that'); it is followed by a circumstantial phrase با تاج بر سر تژاو 'with a crown upon Tazhāv's head'. Notice بر + سر = 'on the head' and تژاو is the possessor placed after سر (classical Persian juxtaposed genitive).
segments.s199-g74: که begins a relative clause linked to the preceding noun (Tazhāv); بودیش is an older compound meaning 'he had it / it belonged to him' (بود + -یش enclitic). The prepositional با introduces the thing possessed (با شیر درنده تاو = 'with the ferocious lion Taav'), where درنده is an adjectival modifier of شیر.
segments.s199-g75: یلانش is یلان + ـش (possessive) = 'his champions/warriors'. همه ('all') modifies the following coordinated nouns نیک‌مردان و شیر ('noblemen and lions'), with و linking them; the phrase enumerates the kinds of men in his host.
segments.s199-g76: که introduces a descriptive clause. هرگز نشدشان is verb + enclitic pronoun (نشد + شان = 'it did not happen to them' / 'their ... did not become'), with دل ('heart') as the patient/subject in this older word order (verb before noun). از رزم سیر is a complement 'sated from battle' (سیر = 'satiated').
segments.s199-g77: بسی here functions adverbially ('many' / 'much' / 'a great number'), modifying برنیامد ('did not come forth / did not rise'); برین روزگار = 'in that/this time' (بر + این/ین + روزگار). The verb برنیامد is negative in sense 'did not appear/come forth'.
segments.s199-g78: که introduces the temporal clause 'when/that'. آن ترک ('that Turk') is the subject; سیر is a predicate adjective 'sated' (placed after the noun in classical word order), and آمد از کارزار is the verb phrase 'came from the battlefield' (آمد = 'came', از کارزار = 'from the battle').
segments.s199-g79: سه بهره (literally 'three shares/portions') is a numeric phrase marking how many units; ز توران = 'from Turan' modifies سپه ('host/army'). کشته شد is the passive construction 'were slain' (کشته + شد as passive auxiliary).
segments.s199-g80: سربخت is an adverbial/nominal expression meaning 'with head raised' or 'proudly'; it precedes the clause آن ترک برگشته شد ('that Turk returned'), where برگشته + شد marks the returned state (برگشته = 'returned', شد as auxiliary). The overall sense is 'that Turk returned, head held high'.
segments.s199-g121: فرود آمد is a past-tense verbal phrase meaning 'alighted/came down' (فرود = alighting, آمد = came). از is the preposition 'from', followed by اسپ 'horse' as the object. او is the third-person pronoun used possessively here ('his'), and اسپنوی is an epithet/appositive attached to اسپ (poetic, no ezāfe) identifying the steed (the name/epithet Aspnavī). Word order (verb before noun phrase) is common in heroic-poetic lines.
segments.s199-g122: تژاو here functions as a noun/epithet (a name or descriptive noun). از غم او is a causal phrase 'because of his grief' (از marking cause), where او refers back to the person in question. پر + از + (N) is the idiom 'full of (N)', so پر از آب روی = 'full of water of the face' i.e. 'face full of tears'; روی is 'face'. The clause states the consequence of grief (tears on the face).
segments.s199-g123: سبکبار شد is a predicative phrase: سبکبار (light‑burdened) + شد 'became' = 'became light of burden'. اسپ is the subject (postposed here for poetic rhythm). و links the second clause; تندی گرفت is an idiom where تندی (haste/speed) + گرفت 'took' = 'took (on) speed' or 'became swift'.
segments.s199-g124: پسش 'پس' = 'after/behind' with the enclitic ش pointing back to the preceding item ('behind it'). The two proper names بیژن گیو are juxtaposed without a conjunction — a common poetic coordination meaning 'Bijan and Giv'. کندی گرفت uses کندی (slowness) + گرفت 'took' in a similar idiom to تندی گرفت: 'took slowness' = 'became slow / restrained' (i.e. they checked or restrained speed). The verb گرفت is the past tense 'took/assumed'.
segments.s199-g125: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; دید is simple past 'saw'. آن introduces the following noun phrase 'that face'. رخ 'face' is modified by ماه‌روی 'moon‑faced' (an adjective), and اسپنوی is an appositive/epithet identifying the steed. Word order places modifiers before the head in poetic phrasing.
segments.s199-g126: ز here is the poetic variant of از meaning 'of/from' or 'by', introducing descriptive material. گلبرگ روی is a metaphorical phrase 'petal-face' = 'a face like a petal' (گلبرگ modifying روی). و links the next description. پر از + مشک موی = 'full of musk (is) the hair' where پر از = 'full of', مشک = 'musk', and موی = 'hair' or 'mane'; the genitive/attributive relations are compact as typical in poetry.
segments.s199-g127: پس 'then' introduces the action. پشت خویش is a possessive phrase 'his back' (پشت + خویش, where خویش is the reflexive/possessive 'his own'); اندرش is اندر + enclitic ش 'in/on it/there' (locative pronoun referring back to the place), and جای کرد is the verbal phrase 'placed' or 'made (a place)' — idiomatically 'set it in place' or 'positioned (himself/it)'. The sequence indicates placing/settling something onto his back.
segments.s199-g128: سوی is 'toward' or 'in the presence of' and introduces the direction/recipient. لشکر پهلوان is a compound noun 'the host/army of champions' or 'the champion-host'. رای کرد is the past tense of رای کردن 'to deliberate/plan/consult' — here 'took counsel' or 'made a decision'. The verb refers back to the subject of the previous clause (he).
segments.s199-g177: سپاهی is a noun meaning 'an army/host' and is followed by ز + a genitive phrase (ز جنگ‌آوران) to indicate 'of/consisting of'. صدهزار is the numeral modifying the army (postposed here in poetic word order) giving the size 'one hundred thousand'.
segments.s199-g178: نهاده is a past participle/perfect of نهادن used here as 'having set/placed' (i.e., 'had set'). همه سر is a collocation 'all heads' (همه = everyone; سر = head, bodily) meaning 'everyone's head'. سوی is the preposition 'toward' and کارزار is 'battlefield' — so the phrase says they had all turned their heads toward the battlefield.
segments.s199-g179: ز دریا بدریا is a poetic pair meaning 'from sea to sea' (بدریا = به دریا 'to the sea'). نبود is the past existential 'there was not', and ایچ (an emphatic negator) means 'at all' or 'nothing'; راه is 'way/road'. Together: 'there was no way at all from sea to sea.'
segments.s199-g180: Here ز is repeated as a preposition meaning 'for/by/from' to list means or agents that could not use the way: اسپ (horse), پیل (elephant), هیون (beast/animal), و سپاه (and an army/host). The repetition gives a distributive 'neither... nor...' sense.
segments.s199-g181: همی is a classical continuous/adverbial particle meaning 'kept on' or 'was doing', used with the past رفت ('went') to form 'kept going' or 'marched on'. لشکر (the army) followed by گروها گروه (a repeated-group construction) means 'groups upon groups' or 'band after band', a plural/distributive expression.
segments.s199-g182: نبد is an archaic negative/existential ('was not' / 'there was not') and inverts the normal predication: 'دشت پیدا نبد' — literally 'the plain was not visible'. The following نه دریا نه کوه ('neither sea nor mountain') lists what could not be seen, reinforcing the lack of any landmark.
segments.s199-g183: بفرمود is a polite/honorific past verb meaning 'he said/commanded'. پیران is the indirect object 'the elders' (those being addressed). که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. بیره is 'the young one(s)' (literally 'lambs, youngsters') and روید here means 'appear' or 'show yourselves' (i.e., come forth).
segments.s199-g184: از ایدر = 'from here' (ایدر = here). سوی راه = 'toward the road/path' and کوته روید combines the adjective کوته 'short' with روید ('go/appear') to mean 'take the shorter route' or 'go by the short way'; روید again carries the sense of 'move forward / show oneself' in this context.
segments.s199-g129: بشادی is an adverbial/prepositional phrase 'with joy' (used before the verb to modify manner). بیامد is past‑tense 3rd singular 'came'. بدرگاه طوس is a locative phrase 'into the court/presence of Tūs' (بـ‑ + درگاه = 'into/at the presence of'; طوس is the proper name). Word order places the adverbial before the verb and the destination after it.
segments.s199-g130: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. درگاه here means 'threshold/gate' (used with ز to mark origin). برخاست is past 3rd singular 'rose/was raised'. آوای کوس (subject) follows the verb in inverted order: 'the sound/voice of the drum' — آوا (sound/voice) governed by کوس (war‑drum).
segments.s199-g131: که introduces a relative clause ('who/which'). بیدار دل is a compound epithet 'alert/awake‑hearted' (بیدار modifies دل). شیر here forms the epithet 'lion‑hearted' modifying دل (دل شیر = 'heart like a lion'). جنگی is an adjectival epithet 'warlike' modifying سوار (rider). The whole sequence lists qualities of the rider referred to by the relative clause.
segments.s199-g132: دمان = 'skirt/edge' (here a locative/destination notion — 'to the skirts/edge'). با is the comitative preposition 'with'. شکار = 'prey/quarry' (the object he brings). آمد is past 3rd singular 'came'. از introduces origin 'from', and مرغزار = 'meadow'. Word order: destination + accompaniment + verb + origin.
segments.s199-g133: سپهدار and گردان are coordinated nouns 'the commander and the troop'. و is the conjunction 'and'. پرخاشجوی is a postpositive adjective 'aggressive/quarrelsome' modifying گردان. This segment names the actors who perform the action in the next segment.
segments.s199-g134: بویرانی + دژ functions as a genitive/attributive phrase 'the fortress of desolation' (a ruined fortress). روی نهادند is an idiom: نهادند (they placed/set) with روی ('face') = 'they set their face toward' = 'they turned (their faces) toward'. Thus the verb phrase means 'they turned toward the ruined fortress.'
segments.s199-g135: ازان پس is an adverbial phrase 'after that / thereupon'. برفتند is past‑tense 3rd plural 'they went/departed'. سوی + noun marks direction 'toward the flock' (سوی گله = 'toward the herd').
segments.s199-g136: که introduces a relative clause modifying گله ('which/that'). بودند is past plural 'they were'. بر دشت ترکان = 'on the plain of the Turks' (بر = on/upon, دشت = plain, ترکان = the Turks). یله is a postpositive adjective 'loose/unrestrained' describing the flock. The clause as a whole: 'which were loose on the Turks' plain.'
segments.s199-g153: بپیران is the prepositional phrase 'ب‑' + پیران meaning 'to the elders' (classical/prepositional b-). ویسه is the speaker (proper name) and چنین precedes the verb as an adverb meaning 'thus/so'. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here used as the main speech verb) and شاه at the end is vocative ('O king'), a common placement in older Persian to address someone after the clause.
segments.s199-g154: که introduces the reported content (a complementizer: 'that' / 'as'), گفتم is first‑person past 'I said' (part of the reported speech), and بیاور is the imperative 'bring'. ز هر سو سپاه is the prepositional phrase 'an army from every side/quarter' with ز as the poetic 'from', هر 'every', سو 'side/quarter', and سپاه 'army/host'.
segments.s199-g155: درنگ (a delay/hesitation) is the noun subject of the clause; آمدت is the past form آمد + second‑person enclitic ‑ت meaning 'came to you' or 'befell you', and رای here means 'counsel/opinion'. از کاهلی is an ablative/'because of' phrase ('from laziness'), giving the cause of the hesitation.
segments.s199-g156: ز is the causal 'because of', پیری 'old age' is the cause, گران گشته is a compound of the participle گشته 'has become' with adjective گران 'heavy/burdensome' ('has grown heavy'), و links to the following noun بددلی 'ill‑will/hardheartedness' — together giving further reasons/conditions.
segments.s199-g157: نه ... نه ... نه is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor ... nor'. دژ 'fortress' is the first item, ماند is the past 'remains/was left', اکنون 'now' gives the time, and اسپ 'steed' and مرد 'man' are the other items in the series.
segments.s199-g158: نشستن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to sit' or 'sitting' used as the subject; نشاید is the impersonal/modal expression 'it is not fitting/it should not be'. بدین مرز is the locative phrase 'on/at this border' (بدین = 'in/at this', مرز = 'border/frontier'), and کرد is the light/lexical verb completing the classical verbal phrase (an archaic periphrastic use meaning essentially 'to do/perform' or used to finish the construction).
segments.s199-g159: بسی here functions as a quantifier 'many' (classical usage), خوی + ش is 'his kin' (خوی = kin/relationship, ‑ش = his), و links the phrase to the next, پیوند ما 'our ties/bonds' (پیوند 'bond/link', ما 'our/we'), برده is the participle 'taken/carried off', and گشت is the past auxiliary 'became/was' completing the passive sense 'were taken'.
segments.s199-g160: بسی again quantifies 'many' (here 'many a'), مرد 'man' is the noun, نیک‌اختر is an epithet/name 'nik‑akhtar' (literally 'good‑starred' — here a proper epithet/name), آزرده 'distressed' is an adjective/participial predicate, and گشت is the past 'became/was' forming 'was distressed'.
segments.s199-g105: This phrase lists two actors plus a prepositional phrase. 'فراز' here functions as a head noun or name modified by the following epithet 'اسپنوی' (a post-nominal adjective/epithet), 'و' links that whole noun phrase to the second actor 'تژاو', and 'از نشیب' is a prepositional phrase ('from the descent/slope') that locates their action.
segments.s199-g106: 'بدو داد' is a transitive past construction: 'داد' (gave/sent) with the enclitic allative pronoun 'بدو' ('to him'). The following adverbial 'در تاختن' ('in/while charging') uses the infinitive/masdar-like form 'تاختن' to indicate the manner/time of the giving/sending, and 'یک رکیب' is the direct object: 'one rival/opponent' sent against him.
segments.s199-g107: 'پس' is a sequential adverb 'then'. 'اندر' ('in/within') begins the locative phrase; the verb 'نشاندش' is the past stem 'نشاند' plus the enclitic object pronoun '-ش' ('him'), meaning 'he seated/placed him'. 'چو' introduces a simile ('like/as'), and 'ماه دمان' is a poetic compound: 'ماه' ('moon' / beautiful one) qualifying 'دمان' ('hem/skirt'), here used figuratively in the simile.
segments.s199-g108: The verb 'برآمد' (arose/issued forth) begins this clause; 'ز جا' ('from the place') marks origin. 'باره' here functions as a collective/occurrence 'a burst or shower' (poetic usage), 'زیرش' = 'beneath him' (زیر + enclitic -ش), and 'دنان' is a poetic noun referring to fine particles/grains (contextually 'dust'). Together they describe dust or a spray rising from beneath him.
segments.s199-g109: 'همی' is a continuous/iterative adverb emphasizing ongoing action. 'تاخت' is the past stem 'galloped/rushed'. 'چون' introduces a simile and 'گرد' here means 'dust' (a dust-cloud simile). 'با اسپنوی' is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with his steed' (با + noun with the epithet 'اسپنوی').
segments.s199-g110: 'سوی راه توران' is a directional phrase: 'toward the road of Turan' ('سوی' = toward, 'راه' = road/path, 'توران' = Turan). The verb 'نهادند' (past, 3rd plural) means 'they placed/put' or here idiomatically 'they set/turned', and 'روی' ('face') completes the expression 'نهادند روی' = 'they turned/set their faces toward' — i.e., they headed in that direction.
segments.s199-g111: 'زمانی' here is an adverb of duration 'for a while'. 'دوید' is the past 'ran'. 'اسپ جنگی' is a noun with a post-nominal adjective: 'اسپ' (horse) + 'جنگی' (warlike / war-horse), and 'تژاو' is the possessor/name attached (Tazhāv's war-horse).
segments.s199-g112: 'نماند' is past negative sense 'there remained / was left'. 'ایچ' is an emphatic negator 'nothing / not at all' (postverbal indefinite). The repeated 'با' ('with') links the two items leftless: 'اسپ' (horse) and 'مرد تاو' (the man Tav); 'مرد' here is 'man/hero' and 'تاو' is the personal name in apposition. The clause means nothing was left of either the horse or the man Tav.
segments.s199-g9: تژاو is an epithet placed before the title and works like an adjectival name (here 'the radiant/brilliant one'); سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (a high military commander). بشد is the classical/perfective narrative 'بـ-شد' meaning 'became/was made'. با سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'with an army' (با = with; سپاه = army/host) indicating accompaniment or command.
segments.s199-g10: بایران here functions as an archaic/locational adjective 'of the barren plain/desert' (used poetically for a waste/plain). خروش آمد is a common epic formula 'a tumult/clamor arose' (خروش = uproar/tumult; آمد = came/ arose). از دیده‌گاه literally 'from the field of sight' — 'into view' or 'within sight' (از = from; دیده‌گاه = viewpoint/field of vision).
segments.s199-g11: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause here meaning 'when/that'; آمد is simple past 'came'. سپاهی operates as a collective 'a force/army' (classical usage), ز ترکان = 'from the Turks' (ز = from), and بجنگ is the verbal/adverbial infinitival sense 'to battle' or 'came to fight' — the whole chunk: 'when an army of Turks came to fight'.
segments.s199-g12: This is a nominal apposition listing the commander and his epithets: سپهبد = 'spahbed' (title). نهنگی is an epithet derived from نهنگ (here used figuratively of great size/strength). درفشی is attributive/agentive 'banner‑bearing' or 'of the standard' (he who bears a derafsh), and پلنگ is an epithet/name of the banner (literally 'leopard' — i.e. his standard bore a leopard or he was called 'Palang'). The sequence is typical epic apposition (title + epithets).
segments.s199-g13: ز گردنکشان marks origin 'from among the rebels' (ز = from; گردنکشان = rebels/proud ones). پیش او رفت is a small clause meaning 'he went before him / approached him' (پیش + pronoun + رفت). Note the subject proper name گیو follows the verb, a common classical word order (verb ... subject).
segments.s199-g14: تنی چند is an idiomatic phrase 'a few (men)' (تنی = 'some/several' here; چند = a few/several). با او = 'with him'. ز گردان = 'from the troop/contingent' (ز = from; گردان = troop). نیو is a short name/epithet attached to گردان (here treated as the troop's name or qualifier); its precise sense is dialectical/poetic but functions as a label for the troop.
segments.s199-g15: برآشفت is a narrative past 'he became angry/was enraged' (compound perfective). و simply links clauses 'and/then'. نامش بپرسید زوی is inverted classical order: نامش = 'his name' (object), بپرسید = 'they asked' (past), ز وی = 'from him' (i.e. they asked his name of him). The whole: 'and when they asked his name of him'.
segments.s199-g16: چنین گفت is the formula 'thus he said' (چنین = thus). کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone (not the royal title here). مرد = 'man' and پرخاشجوی is an adjective 'quarrelsome/aggressive' forming the vocative phrase 'O quarrelsome man' — what he utters in reply. Note: گفت here functions as the speech‑verb introducing the quoted vocative.
segments.s199-g33: بدین مایه لشکر تو تندی مجوی is a short clause meaning 'with this amount/force, (your) army, do not seek haste.' بدین is a classical prepositional phrase ('با + این' → 'with this') placed before the noun it modifies; مایه = 'measure/means/force' and links to لشکر تو ('your army') as the unit being described. تندی مجوی is a negative subjunctive/imperative: مجوی = م + جوی (the prohibitive/negative prefix م attached to the present/subjunctive stem جوی 'seek'), so 'do not seek haste' or 'do not look for speed.'
segments.s199-g34: بتندی بپیش دلیران مپوی continues the prohibition: بتندی here is an adverbial/verb form from تند (to hurry, to be swift) used as 'with haste / hasten'; بپیش = به + پیش 'toward / before (the presence of)'; دلیران is the plural adjective/noun 'the brave' (object of movement); مپوی is another negative form (م + پوی, from پوییدن/پوییدن 'go/run'), meaning 'do not go/run' or 'do not rush.' Together it says 'and do not hasten forward toward the brave.'
segments.s199-g35: که این پرهنر نامدار دلیر begins with که introducing a subordinate/explanatory clause ('that' / 'for'), followed by the demonstrative این 'this' and three stacked epithets (پرهنر 'skilled', نامدار 'renowned', دلیر 'brave'). In Classical Persian the descriptive adjectives often follow the demonstrative and jointly modify an implied noun ('this skilled, famed, brave one').
segments.s199-g36: سر مرزبان اندر آرد بزیر is the predicate of the clause: آرد is a 3rd-person singular present/future-form of آوردن 'bring' (used narratively as 'will bring' or 'brings'); سر مرزبان is the direct object 'the marzban's head' (classical word order often omits the ezafe/را); اندر is a locative/prepositional particle 'into/within' and together with بزیر 'down' (adverbial) completes the verb: 'bring (the) head of the marzban down.' The word order is inverted for poetic effect but reads as 'he will bring the marzban's head down.'
segments.s199-g37: گر اایدونک فرمان کنی با سپاه is a conditional protasis: گر = 'if'; اایدونک is a vocative/form of address ('O little one' / 'O youth'); فرمان کنی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive form of کردن after گر meaning 'you issue/order' (classical Persian often uses کنی for 'you should/might do'); با سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'with an army' (با + سپاه). The clause reads 'If, O youth, you give the command with the army...'.
segments.s199-g38: بایران خرامی بنزدیک شاه gives the consequent action/instruction. The sequence expresses movement toward the king: خرامی is a verbal noun/adverb from خرامیدن 'to stride proudly' ('in proud stride'); بنزدیک = ب + نزدیک 'to/near' (directional 'up close to'); شاه is the goal 'the king'. The first item (بایران) appears as a single token here but does not fit the supplied sense list cleanly (it likely intends 'با ایران' or a poetic form meaning 'with the Iranians' or 'with the army/host'); overall: 'go (with them) striding proudly up close to the king.'
segments.s199-g39: کنون پیش طوس سپهبد شوی is an imperative/instructional clause: کنون 'now' sets the time; پیش طوس = 'in the presence of Tous' (پیش + proper name طوس); سپهبد شوی is a 2nd-person singular subjunctive/optative of شدن 'become' with the predicate سپهبد 'spahbed/commander' — 'become spahbed' or 'take the rank of spahbed.' Word order places the predicate before the verb as typical in Persian.
segments.s199-g40: بگویی و گفتار او بشنوی is two coordinated clauses: بگویی (from گفتن) is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive 'you shall speak' or 'you will speak'; و = 'and'; گفتار او بشنوی = 'you hear his speech' (بشنوی = ب + شنوی, present/subjunctive of شنیدن 'to hear'). In context it means 'speak, and listen to what he says' or 'you will speak and listen to his words.'
segments.s199-g185: نباید is an impersonal negative modal ('should not / must not') and is followed by the subordinating که introducing the subordinate clause. یابند is the classical-subjunctive/optative 3rd‑person plural form of 'to find/obtain' (here: 'they should come to obtain'). خود is reflexive/emphatic ('themselves' / 'for themselves') modifying آگهی, which here means 'news/information'. The phrase therefore means 'they should not themselves obtain news'.
segments.s199-g186: ازین = از + این ('from/of these'); نامداران is a plural noun 'the renowned/illustrious ones' (object of آگهی: 'news about these men'). با is the preposition 'with/by', and فرهی (adjective/noun) here denotes 'nobility/ distinction' and qualifies the way the news would be known. Together: 'news about these renowned men with/and their nobility'.
segments.s199-g187: مگر functions here as 'unless / except' introducing a conditional/adversative clause. ناگهان is an adverb 'suddenly' modifying the coming verb. بر + سر + آن + گروه is the prepositional phrase 'upon that group' (سر used idiomatically to mean 'upon / against' a troop).
segments.s199-g188: فرود آرم is a classical verbal phrase: فرود (descent/bringing down) + آرم, where آرم is an optative/1st‑person form ('may I bring / I will bring down' — here a threat/possibility). این گشن لشکر is the object 'this hungry army/host' and چو کوه is a simile 'like a mountain'. Word order places the verbal phrase before its object and simile.
segments.s199-g189: برون کرد combines the adverb برون ('out, forth') with کرد ('did, brought') to mean 'brought out' / 'rushed forth'. کارآگهان (from کارآگه) is an agent noun 'the informed/knowing one' and functions as subject; ناگهان is the adverb 'suddenly' emphasizing the suddenness of the action.
segments.s199-g190: همی is the classical continuous/progressive particle ('was / kept / then'), and جست is the verb 'leapt/sprang'. بیدار modifies کار جهان ('the affairs of the world') and here is best rendered as 'awakened' or 'alert' — so the phrase means 'the affairs of the world sprang awake' with the verb before the noun phrase (typical poetic order).
segments.s199-g191: بتندی is a classical present (or preterite‑present) verb formed with the prefix ب- and the root تندیدن meaning 'to hasten' (here: 'he hastened'). براه ('on the way') is an adverbial phrase. اندر آورد روی is a light-verbal construction where آورد ('brought/turned') takes روی ('face') as its object: 'he turned his face (inward/toward)'. The sequence shows usual Persian verb‑object ordering in poetry.
segments.s199-g192: بسوی is a directional preposition 'toward'; گروگرد is 'the crowd/throng' (object of the motion). شد is the past/preterite of شدن 'became' and here expresses taking on a role or posture; جنگجوی (warrior) is the predicate noun: 'he became/appeared as a warrior toward the throng' — i.e. he advanced upon them as a fighter.
segments.s199-g89: بیفگند نیزه بیازید چنگ is a compact, poetic clause: بیفگند (simple past, third-person singular) = 'he threw/ hurled'; نیزه is the direct object 'spear'; بیازید (here present/Perfectic-inchoative sense in older Persian) = 'gaped/opened'; چنگ = 'the claw' or 'talon'. The three-part sequence therefore describes two linked actions: he cast the spear, and the (animal's) claw opened.
segments.s199-g90: چو ... تازد پلنگ is a comparative adverbial clause introduced by چو = 'when/as'. The internal order is poetic: بر کوه بر غرم تازد پلنگ = 'the panther rushes upon the mountain in (its) wrath' where بر + کوه = 'upon the mountain', بر + غرم = 'in/with wrath', تازد is present/habitual 'rushes', and پلنگ is subject 'the panther'. The whole clause supplies the simile for the preceding action.
segments.s199-g91: بدان سان که is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'in that way/so that'; it is built from ب + دان + سان + که and functions as a comparative introducer. شاهین (subject 'the hawk') + رباید (present/habitual 'seizes/snatches') + چکاو (object 'the lark') is the example used by the comparison.
segments.s199-g92: ربود آن گرانمایه تاج تژاو is a straightforward past-action clause: ربود (simple past) = 'he took/seized'; آن = 'that/the'; گرانمایه = attributive adjective 'precious/valuable'; تاج = 'crown'; تژاو = an epithet meaning 'radiant/bright' qualifying the crown. Together: 'he took that precious, radiant crown.'
segments.s199-g93: که introduces a subordinate temporal clause 'when/that'. افراسیابش = 'Afrasiab' with a clitic referring to the object just taken; بسر برنهاد is an older/poetic expression meaning 'placed on the head' (برنهاد = 'set/placed on', بسر = 'on the head' in this idiom). The whole segment = 'when Afrasiab set it on his head.'
segments.s199-g94: نبودی جدا زو بخواب و بیاد is negative-past + ablative phrase: نبودی = 'was not' (past), جدا زو = 'separate/apart from it' (زو = from it), and بخواب و بیاد names two states 'in sleep and in memory/awareness'. Together the clause says he did not part from it, neither in sleep nor in memory (i.e. he kept it constantly).
segments.s199-g95: چنین تا در دژ همی تاخت اسپ is a narrative clause: چنین = 'thus/so'; تا ... همی تاخت = 'until / kept on galloping' with همی marking continuous/habitual aspect; در دژ = 'into/in the fortress'; اسپ = 'the horse/steed' (subject). The sequence reports the horse's continuous charge into the citadel.
segments.s199-g96: پس‌اندرش = 'thereupon/after that (in it)' (a temporal connective), بیژن is the subject 'Bijan', چو = 'as/like', and آذرگشسپ is a proper name used as a simile. The short clause means 'thereafter Bijan (acted) like Azar-Gushasp' (i.e. he followed/entered in the same heroic manner).
segments.s199-g169: سوی is a preposition meaning 'toward' placed before its complement. میمنه here means 'the right (side/hand)' and forms the directional phrase سوی میمنه 'toward the right'. بارمان و تژاو is a coordinated noun phrase following that directional phrase (two names or epithets joined by و); there is no linking ezāfe because this is a prepositional object in poetic word-order.
segments.s199-g170: سواران is the head noun 'riders' (subject). که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. دارند is the present indicative 'they have/are carrying' and governs the remainder of the clause; با is the preposition 'with', شیر is the noun 'lion', and تاو is used here as a name/epithet attached to شیر. Word order: subject + relative clause (که + verb + complement).
segments.s199-g171: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. نستهین functions here as a verbal form referring to the assembled/sat/clustered action (poetic/archaic verb form). گرد بر is a prepositional/adverbial sequence 'around/on' (گرد = around, بر = on/upon) and میسره names the place/setting phrased after those particles. The whole segment gives the temporal/location setting for the next clause.
segments.s199-g172: کجا acts as a relative/adverbial 'where'. شیر is the subject 'the lion'. بودی is an old/poetic past form 'was' (past tense). بچنگش is a compound prepositional/locative form meaning 'into his grasp' (بـ + چنگ + ـش), and بره is the direct object 'the lamb'. The clause follows the pattern 'where X was, Y came into his grip' with the past-tense verb linking the two parts.
segments.s199-g173: جهان is the subject 'the world'. پر شد is the passive/resultative phrase 'was filled' (شد as perfective/become plus پر 'full'). از introduces the source/cause 'with/from' and links to the following noun phrase ناله کرنای, where ناله (lament/cry) is specified by کرنای 'of the horn/trumpet' in a genitive/construct relationship.
segments.s199-g174: ز (poetic variant of از) marks cause/means 'from' or 'because of'. غریدن is a verbal noun/infinitive here 'the roaring/roar(ing)'. کوس is the noun 'war-drum' that is the agent of that roaring. و links an additional modifier هندی; درای follows as a descriptive element. Grammatically this segment gives additional causes of the world's being filled: 'from the roaring of the drum and the (Indian) ...' (the exact instrumental noun is expressed elliptically in poetry).
segments.s199-g175: هوا is the noun 'the air/sky'. سربسر is an adverbial 'throughout / everywhere'. The three color adjectives سرخ، زرد، بنفش follow and enumerate the hues seen; in Persian descriptive adjectives commonly follow the noun, and the copula ('is/was') is omitted in poetic style, so this reads as 'the sky everywhere red and yellow and purple'.
segments.s199-g176: ز 'from' introduces the cause: بس here conveys abundance ('so many/so much'), followed by نیزه 'spears' and the coordinating و. گونه‌گونه is a distributive/adjectival phrase 'various / manifold' modifying درفش 'banners/standards'. Together the phrase means 'from so many spears and banners of various kinds' giving the visual cause of the colored sky.
segments.s199-g17: بدین مایه = 'with this/these means' is an adverbial phrase (بدین = 'with this' + مایه = 'means, resource'). مردم بجنگ آمدی is verb-final: مردم (object/target 'the people') + بجنگ آمدی ('you came to fight' — آمدی is 2nd-person past 'you came', بجنگ is the verbal prefix+noun 'to fight'). The whole segment = 'with these means you came to fight the people.'
segments.s199-g18: ز هامون بکام نهنگ آمدی literally orders as: ز هامون ('from the plain') + بکام نهنگ ('to/for the whale's liking'—بکام is a prepositional adjective meaning 'to the desire/liking of') + آمدی ('you came', past 2nd person). The clause is a second coordinate: 'and/from the plain you came, pleasing to the whale.'
segments.s199-g19: بپاسخ چنین گفت is a formulaic reply: بپاسخ (in answer / as a reply) + چنین ('thus/so') + گفت (said). کای نامدار is a vocative: کای = 'O' (addressing someone) + نامدار = 'renowned'. The whole segment = 'In reply he said: "O renowned one..."'.
segments.s199-g20: ببینی کنون رزم شیر سوار is a clause where ببینی is a 2nd-person subjunctive/optative or hortative form ('may you see' / 'you shall see') used after گفت; کنون = 'now'; رزم = 'battle'; شیر سوار = a compound 'lion-rider' (شیر 'lion' + سوار 'rider'). Word order places the verb-form first (typical in classical poetry), so: 'Now you shall see the battle of the lion‑rider.'
segments.s199-g21: بگیتی تژاوست نام مرا reads: بگیتی ('in the world' or 'among people') + تژاوست (here functioning as a proper name or archaic word—appears as the name being asserted) + نام مرا ('my name' where مرا is the possessive/object). The clause order is topical (place/time first) then name then 'name of me'.
segments.s199-g22: بهر دم برآرند کام مرا: بهر دم ('for each breath' = 'at every moment') + برآرند (3rd-person plural present/subjunctive meaning 'they bring about/fulfil') + کام مرا ('my desire' with مرا as possessive). The verb is plural 'they' and governs کام مرا as its object: 'they fulfil my desire at every moment.'
segments.s199-g23: نژادم بگوهر از ایران بدست = نژادم ('my lineage' — نژاد + ضمیر) + بگوهر (predicate/adjective 'set with gems' or 'adorned like a jewel') + از ایران ('from Iran') + بدست (here 'by' / 'obtained by'). The order places the possessive lineage first, then its description and origin.
segments.s199-g24: ز گردان وز پشت شیران بدست is a continuation of the agent/source phrase: ز گردان ('from/through the troop') + وز (here likely an archaic conjunction or mis-tokenization; the given lexical senses don't match, so contextually it functions like 'and/from') + پشت شیران ('on the backs/with the support of the lions' — شیران used figuratively for 'brave men' or literally 'lions') + بدست (repeated 'obtained/by'). The phrase lists the forces by which the lineage/status was acquired.
